title: Fixing Invalid Node Traversal in a Binary Tree

statement.markdown: Given a binary tree, implement a function to traverse the tree in an in-order manner. The function should print each node's value in the order of left subtree, root, right subtree.
statement.inputFormat: The input will be a binary tree represented as a linked list of nodes, where each node contains a value and references to its left and right children.
statement.outputFormat: The output should be a list of node values in in-order traversal order.
statement.notes: The binary tree nodes have the following structure: `class Node { int val; Node left, right; }`. The node values are integers.

examples.explanation:
In-order traversal of the following binary tree: 4 
 / \ 2   5 / \ 2   3 3

starterCode.cpp: class Solution { public: vector<int> solve(Node* root) { // TODO: implement } };
starterCode.java: class Solution { public: List<Integer> solve(Node root) { // TODO: implement } };
starterCode.python: class Solution: def solve(self, root: TreeNode) -> List[int]: # TODO: implement

buggyStarterCode.cpp: class Solution { public: vector<int> solve(Node* root) { if (root) { return solve(root->left) + solve(root->right); } return vector<int>(); } };
buggyStarterCode.java: class Solution { public: List<Integer> solve(Node root) { if (root != null) { List<Integer> left = solve(root.left); List<Integer> right = solve(root.right); left.addAll(right); return left; } return new ArrayList<>(); } };
buggyStarterCode.python: class Solution: def solve(self, root: TreeNode) -> List[int]: if root: return self.solve(root.left) + self.solve(root.right); return [];
buggyStarterCode.javascript: class Solution { solve(root) { if (root) { let left = self.solve(root.left); let right = self.solve(root.right); return left.concat(right); } return []; } }

testCases.input:
5
1 2 3 4 5 null null null 2 3 null null 4 5 null null
